title: Jacket potato with cheese and baked beans
subtitle: A properly baked potato with melted cheddar and hot beans
excerpt: A jacket potato done properly takes time but almost no skill: pricked, rubbed with oil and salt, and baked in a hot oven until the skin is crisp and the flesh is fluffy. Topped with grated cheddar and baked beans, it is one of the most satisfying meals in British cooking.
sourceNotes: Standard British jacket potato method. Potato baking technique draws on widely agreed British practice of high-heat baking for a crisp skin. Baked beans pairing is characteristic of the British luncheon and supper tradition from the mid-20th century onward.

content.text: A jacket potato with cheese and beans is one of the best things you can make with very little effort. The key is using a large, floury potato such as a Maris Piper or King Edward, rubbing the skin with oil and flaked salt, and baking it at a high temperature until the outside is crackly and the inside fluffy. You cannot rush it.

items.prepNote: large, floury variety (Maris Piper or King Edward), about 300 g each

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Scrub the potatoes and dry them well. Prick each one all over with a fork, about 20 times.
Rub each potato all over with oil, then with a generous pinch of sea salt flakes. Place directly on the oven rack.
Bake for 70 to 75 minutes until the skin is crisp and you can push a knife through the centre with no resistance. The skin should feel hard and crackling when you tap it.
Heat the baked beans in a small saucepan over a medium heat, stirring occasionally.
Cut a deep cross into the top of each potato and squeeze the ends to push the flesh up and open. Add butter if using. Spoon on the beans and pile with the grated cheddar. Season with black pepper.

items.symptom: The skin is soft, not crisp
items.cause: The oven temperature was too low, or the potato was not dried before oiling.
items.fix: Dry the potato thoroughly before rubbing with oil. Bake at a high temperature for the full time. Wrapping in foil, as some recipes suggest, steams the skin and prevents crisping.

items.symptom: The flesh is still dense and starchy
items.cause: Not enough time in the oven, or the wrong variety of potato was used.
items.fix: Use a floury variety and bake until a knife pushes through with absolutely no resistance. This can take up to 90 minutes for very large potatoes.

items.symptom: The potato explodes in the oven
items.cause: It was not pricked before baking.
items.fix: Always prick generously. Steam builds inside and needs to escape.

content.text: The jacket potato is the backbone of British lunchtime eating. It is found in every street stall, school canteen, and home kitchen in the country. The cheese-and-beans combination became standard from the 1950s. It is as distinctly British as fish and chips, and considerably easier to make.
